Avellanda Stoikov framework

#market_making #paper_explained

The seminal paper of Avellanda-Stoikov kickstarted the stochastic control approach to market making problems. In the following post we summarise the main feature of the model and comment on the various steps. We are interested in the market making problem, that is to provide liquidity to the markets via the limit orderbook at all points in time. To approach this problem we will make some assumptions about the evolution of the system (asset price, market order arrivals, etc.) and derive and solve the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation.

Notation and assumptions

We set each trading period to start at time $0$ and end at time $T$. We assume the mid-price to follow an arithmetic brownian motion with constant volatility, i.e. \[dS_t = \sigma dW_t.\] It is known that the distribution of asset returns is not Gaussian and that it has fat tails. This implies that this assumption does not allow the model to capture jumps that are commonly observed in real market data.
We are interested in posting on both sides of the orderbook and we denote by $\delta^a_t$, $\delta^b_t$ the price difference between our the mid-price and our ask and bid order respectively. This means that we are posting a sell and buy order at the prices \[p^a_t = S_t + \delta^a_t, \ p^b_t = S_t - \delta^b_t. \] We want to simulate our orders being executed using probabilities (as in this world there is no certainty). We model this intuition by assuming limit order execution to follow a Poisson process with intensities \[\lambda(delta^a_t), \lambda(delta^b_t).\] It makes sense that the further away the order is from the mid-price, the lower the probability that it will be executed when the next market order comes. This amounts to require $\lambda^a, \ \lambda^b$ to be decreasing. The research on price impact gives us some valid guesses about the functional form for these two functions. Avellanda and Stoikov chose a an exponential for the intensities. All the orders on the book are assumed to have constant size $1$. The model does not allow for multiple orders to be open on the same side at the same time. We denote the inventory by $q_t$. The inventory is important for market makers because it is a quantity related to their exposure directional risk. Naturally, the market maker wants to keep the inventory as small as possible.
The variable $q_t$ changes as orders get executed. Let $N^a_t$, $N^b_t$ denote the stochastic processes counting the number of orders executed on the ask and bid side respectively. We have the relationship \[q_t = N_t^b - N_t^a.\] Another process we keep track of is the cash balance $X_t$ defined as \[X_t = (S_t + \delta_t^a) * N_t^a - (S_t-\delta_t^b) * N_t^b.\] In AS the market making problem is formulated as a minimization problem. We introduce the utility functional \[H(x, s, q, t) = max_{delta_^a, delta_b}\mathbb{E}\left[- \exp(-\gamma(X_T - q_TS_T)\vert X_t=x\right],\] where $\gamma$ is a constant related to the risk aversion of the market maker (how?). We notice that the argument of the expected value is negative, and its value grows with the argument of the exponential. So to maximize this functional we want the sum of the final cash balance and final cash value of the inventory to be large. Solving this problem will give us the optimal quoting on the average price trajectory with initial point $x$. We notice that the amount $q_TS_T$ corresponds to an actual exposure to the asset price. This is not necessarily what we want, as it can incentivize building large positions. Some subsequent papers explicitly penalize building large inventories (like Lehalle).
The standard way to approach the problem is to compute the HJB equation and look for a solution to that equation (and then prove that it is indeed a maximizer). The HJB is the infinitesimal version of the Dynamic programming principle. The Dynamic programming principle states that if you are facing an optimization problem on a trajectory and somebody tells you how to act optimally from time $\bar{4}$ onwards, you just need to figure out how to act optimally from the current time until $\bar{t}$. So the DPP gives an iterative way to solve the optimization problem by splitting it into subproblems that look similar to the original problem. The HJB is obtained by applying the DPE until some time $t + h$ and letting $h$ approach $0$.
To get the HJB equation in practice it is sufficient to know how to differentiate w.r.t. the stochastic trajectories we are following. In our specific case we have two stochastic processes, the brownian motion for the price and the poisson point process for the executions. The motion of our systems follows the two dimensional trajectory (W_t, N_t). On this trajectory we are computing some quantities of interest (like the amount of cash), the opimization problem depends on. So computing the derivative of $H$ w.r.t. to $W_t$ and $N_t$ will give us the HJB pde.
The derivative w.r.t. to $W_t$ is given by Ito’s lemma and in this case it reduces to \[ADD.\] Taking the derivative w.r.t. $N_t$ has the effect of focusing on the jump times, i.e. the times when $N_t^- \neq N_t$. We consider the change of $H$ during these jump times.
